text: In these strange, angled spaces of glass and neon, something about the environment feels both alien and inviting. The first view, with its reflections and hard surfaces, suggests that I am on the threshold of something artificial—an experiment in interaction. Stepping inside, I find the words on the wall compelling: 'Confusion is indeed the rhythm.' I feel like a participant and an observer at once, swept up in the playful uncertainty of interpretation. Here, confusion isn’t a flaw but a feature—a necessary haze through which new meaning might emerge. It is oddly comforting, the idea that what I make of this place will only come together with time.
